summary: The final imperial dynasty of China (1644-1912), founded by Manchu conquerors of the Aisin Gioro clan who overthrew the Ming dynasty and were in turn overthrown by the Xinhai Revolution, which forced the abdication of the child emperor Puyi. Its greatest rulers, the Kangxi and Qianlong Emperors, expanded the empire through campaigns against the Dzungar Khanate and suppressed internal revolts like the Revolt of the Three Feudatories, while its final century was marked by the Taiping Rebellion, a series of 'unequal treaties' forced on it after military defeats by Western powers, the Boxer Rebellion, and the dominant regency of the Empress Dowager Cixi. It is also remembered for Jingdezhen porcelain in styles like famille rose, and for court painters like the Jesuit Giuseppe Castiglione.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How the dynasty is identified in giveaways: the final imperial dynasty of China, founded by Manchu conquerors who succeeded the Ming dynasty and ruled from 1644 to 1912. Its most celebrated rulers were the Kangxi and Qianlong Emperors, and it ended when the last emperor, Puyi, abdicated in the wake of the Xinhai Revolution.

clues.clue: The final dynasty of China, founded by Manchu conquerors and supplanting the Ming dynasty
examples: The Taiping Rebellion occurred during, for 10 points, what final dynasty of China ruled by the Manchus?
Kangxi was a ruler of this final Chinese dynasty, which supplanted the Ming.

clues.clue: Ruled by the Kangxi and Qianlong Emperors, its most celebrated rulers
examples: Hong Taiji founded this dynasty, which was ruled by the Kangxi and Qianlong emperors.
The Kangxi and Qianlong Emperors were rulers from this Manchu imperial dynasty, the last to rule China.

clues.clue: The dynasty ended in 1912 when the last emperor, Puyi, abdicated following the success of the Xinhai Revolution
examples: Puyi abdicated the throne after-for 10 points-what revolution that established the Republic of China?

works.name: Manchu Conquest and the Founding of the Dynasty
works.description: The dynasty grew out of the Jurchen chieftain Nurhaci's unification of his people, who were organized into the Eight Banners and later renamed 'Manchu' by his successor Hong Taiji, the dynasty's actual founder. Its conquest of China proper came in 1644, when the Ming general Wu Sangui let Manchu forces through Shanhai Pass to crush the rebel Li Zicheng, after which the new rulers imposed the queue hairstyle on Han Chinese men as a mark of submission.

clues.clue: The Jurchen chieftain Nurhaci, of the Aisin Gioro clan, issued his 'Seven Grievances' against the Ming and won the Battle of Sarhu, having organized his people (divided by Ming sources into the Haixi, Jianzhou, and a 'wild' group) into the Eight Banners military system
examples: These people won the Battle of Sarhu during a conflict preceded by their leader issuing his Seven Grievances.
Ming-era sources divide these people into three groups: the Haixi, Jianzhou, and a "wild" one.

clues.clue: Nurhaci was wounded in a later defeat at the Battle of Ningyuan and died soon after
examples: The basis for the Qing dynasty and the Eight Banners system can be accredited to this Jurchen chieftain, who died soon after his defeat in the Battle of Ningyuan.

clues.clue: Nurhaci's successor Hong Taiji renamed the Jurchen people 'Manchu' and founded the dynasty
examples: Hong Taiji adopted a new name for these people, who were led by members of the Aisin Gioro clan beginning with Nurhaci.
Hong Taiji founded this dynasty, which was ruled by the Kangxi and Qianlong emperors.

clues.clue: The Manchus sent envoys south to form the 'Alliance Conducted at Sea,' which split conquests from a proposed joint invasion
examples: These people sent envoys sailing south to form the Alliance Conducted at Sea, which split conquests from a proposed invasion.

clues.clue: After the rebel Li Zicheng ('the Dashing Prince') sacked Beijing, the Ming general Wu Sangui let Manchu forces through Shanhai Pass, bypassing the Great Wall, where they defeated Li Zicheng's army in 1644 under the regent Prince Dorgon; Wu Sangui then accepted Manchu suzerainty
examples: A paradigmatic traitor opened a pass's gates to this empire's regent Dorgon in 1644.
Wu basically let the Manchus into China by allowing to get past this several-thousand-mile-long edifice, which was built to keep pesky northern ruffians like the Manchus out.

clues.clue: The new dynasty forced Han Chinese men to wear the queue hairstyle to enforce assimilation, under the slogan 'keep your head, lose your hair; keep your hair, lose your head'; the city of Jiangyin resisted the mandate for two and a half months before being massacred after surrendering
examples: Name this dynasty, which forced all Han men to wear the queue to force assimilation to Manchu customs.

This city along the lower Yangtze rebelled for two and a half months in resistance to the queue mandate. After surrendering by dropping hair clippings over the city walls, its population was massacred.

works.name: The Kangxi Emperor and the Revolt of the Three Feudatories
works.description: The Kangxi Emperor ruled from 1661 to 1722, commissioning a namesake dictionary and a massive encyclopedia. In 1673 he faced the Revolt of the Three Feudatories, an uprising by Wu Sangui and two other enfeoffed lords who refused to surrender their lands; he also warred against the Dzungar Khanate and, in 1689, signed the dynasty's first treaty with a European power at Nerchinsk.

clues.clue: The Kangxi Emperor ruled from 1661 until 1722, when he was succeeded by the Yongzheng Emperor, and commissioned a namesake dictionary and the encyclopedia A Collection of Books and Illustrations of Ancient and Modern Times
examples: Identify this man who was succeeded by the Yongzheng Emperor after ruling from 1661 until 1722.
One ruler of this dynasty contended with linguistic radicals codified in his namesake (*) dictionary and political radicals in the Revolt of the Three Feudatories.

clues.clue: In 1673, Kangxi faced the Revolt of the Three Feudatories, led by Wu Sangui (the most capable of the three, based in Sichuan, who had defected to the Qing after the rebel Li Zicheng executed his father and favorite concubine), Geng Jingzhong, and Shang Zhixin, lords who refused to surrender their vast enfeoffed lands; the Eight Banners were initially outperformed by the mostly Han Green Standard Army during the revolt
examples: In 1673, Kangxi was faced with this uprising led by Wu Sangui, Geng Jingzhong, and Shang Zhixin. Those lords had been enfeoffed with a huge amount of land and refused to give it up to Kangxi.

The Eight Banners of the Qing army were initially outperformed by the mostly Han Green Standard Army during this conflict. Wu Sangui led one faction during this rebellion after resigning his Qing governorship.

clues.clue: Kangxi revitalized the baojia mutual-surveillance system, which grouped households into units of ten and a hundred, to suppress the local population
examples: The baojia system was revitalized by this dynasty's Kangxi emperor, who instead used it to suppress the local population. This dynasty was established in 1644 by Manchus invading the Ming dynasty.

clues.clue: Kangxi signed the Treaty of Nerchinsk (1689) with Russia, the dynasty's first treaty with a European power, fixing much of the empire's northern border; the Chinese Rites Controversy and Jesuit missions to his court are also associated with his reign
examples: Name this dynasty that concluded its first treaty with a European power when it signed the Treaty of Nerchinsk with Russia.

This Qing emperor suppressed the Revolt of the Three Feudatories. The Chinese Rites Controversy occurred while Matteo Ricci was a visitor at this man's court, and the Treaty of Nerchinsk was signed during this man's reign.

clues.clue: The Treaty of Nerchinsk was reaffirmed and updated by the 1727 Treaty of Kyakhta, signed in a new town on the eastern terminus of the Siberian Route from Moscow, under which the dynasty agreed to tolerate the Orthodox Church
examples: The Treaty of Nerchinsk was reaffirmed and updated in this 1727 treaty, which was signed in a newly founded town that served as the eastern terminus of the Siberian Route from Moscow.
This country agreed to tolerate the Orthodox Church by terms of the Treaty of Kyakhta.

clues.clue: Kangxi warred against the Dzungar Khanate, an Oirat state led by Galdan that at its height ruled much of Xinjiang; Dzungar forces fled the Battle of Ulan Butung after forming a 'camel wall' of 10,000 tied-together camels, and Galdan was later defeated at Dzuunmod
examples: Forces under this state fled from the Battle of Ulan Butung after forming a "camel wall" by tying 10,000 of the ungulates together and firing from between them.
The Dzungar Khanate declined after a war with this Chinese emperor, who signed the 1689 treaty of Nerchinsk with Peter III and commissioned a namesake dictionary.

works.name: The Qianlong Emperor and the Ten Great Campaigns
works.description: Kangxi's grandson the Qianlong Emperor restricted the Eight Banners to Manchus and, during his Ten Great Campaigns, waged what one genocide scholar called an 18th-century genocide against the Dzungars as part of a 'Pacification of Xinjiang,' alongside less successful invasions of Burma and Vietnam. His reign also saw the rejected Macartney Embassy and the rise of his notoriously corrupt favorite Heshen, and he abdicated in 1796 to avoid surpassing his grandfather's reign length.

clues.clue: The Qianlong Emperor, grandson of Kangxi, restricted membership in the Eight Banner System to those of Manchu descent
examples: Name this emperor who restricted membership in the Eight Banner System to those of Manchu descent and was the grandson of the Kangxi Emperor.

clues.clue: During the Ten Great Campaigns, Qianlong's forces waged what genocide scholar Mark Levene called 'the 18th-century genocide par excellence' against the Dzungars, part of a 'Pacification of Xinjiang' celebrated by the Puning Temple at Chengde; the Dzungars had previously ruled and heavily taxed the Muslim Uyghur people, who now inhabit the 'autonomous region' of Xinjiang
examples: Perdue has accused one ruler of this dynasty of waging genocide against the Dzungar people during an expedition that also saw the "Pacification of Xinjiang," which was celebrated by the construction of the Puning Temple in Chengde.

Name these peoples who were exterminated by the Qianlong Emperor's forces in his Ten Great Campaigns. Mark Levene, a genocide scholar, described the atrocity as "the 18th-century genocide par excellence."

clues.clue: The Ten Great Campaigns also included less successful invasions of Burma and Vietnam; Xinjiang was later organized as a province in 1884, under the dynasty's Guangxu Emperor
examples: That ruler of this dynasty also saw less successful invasions of Burma and Vietnam during his Ten Great Campaigns.
Xinjiang was turned into a province in 1884 under this dynasty's Guangxu Emperor.

clues.clue: Qianlong employed Michel Benoist and the Jesuit Giuseppe Castiglione to help build a summer palace
examples: This dynasty employed Michel Benoist and Giuseppe Castiglione to help build a summer palace.

clues.clue: The 1793 Macartney Embassy, a British diplomatic mission, was hosted by Qianlong's corrupt favorite Heshen (who acquired up to 1.1 million taels of silver as a director of the Siku Quanshu) but was largely rejected
examples: Name this 1793 British embassy whose overtures were largely rejected by the Qianlong emperor.
The embassy was hosted by this legendarily corrupt official who acquired up to 1.1 million taels of silver. He was forced to commit suicide after his patron Qianlong abdicated.

clues.clue: Heshen's corruption came to exemplify the dynasty's decline after Qianlong's rule; Qianlong abdicated in 1796 to avoid surpassing the reign length of his grandfather Kangxi, remaining in de facto power until 1799
examples: Heshen has been considered emblematic of the corruption which led to the decline of this dynasty after the rule of Qianlong.

The Qing conquered the Dzungars and captured Mongolia during the Ten Great Campaigns of this emperor, who abdicated in 1796 to avoid reigning longer than the Kangxi Emperor but remained in de facto power until 1799.

works.name: Foreign Trade, Missionaries, and the Unequal Treaties
works.description: Jesuit missionaries like Matteo Ricci sparked the Chinese Rites Controversy over Confucian ancestor veneration, while later Protestant missions like Hudson Taylor's China Inland Mission also operated in the country. American traders such as Peter Parker, James Flint, and the firm Russell & Co. competed for access, and a series of treaties with the US and other powers — increasingly 'unequal' after military defeats like the Opium Wars — reshaped the dynasty's foreign relations, culminating in the Boxer Rebellion and invasion by the Eight-Power Alliance.

clues.clue: Catholics were banned from displaying 'seat of the soul' ancestor tablets, and Jesuit missionaries like Matteo Ricci led missions before decrees by Pope Clement XI (issued at the urging of Franciscans and Dominicans) ended the Chinese Rites Controversy over whether ancestor veneration was compatible with Christianity
examples: Decrees by Pope Clement XI issued at the behest of Franciscans and Dominicans ended a controversy named for (*) rites in this country, where most bishops operate underground.
Name this country that names a "rites controversy" in which Catholic missionaries argued a local tradition was compatible with Christianity.

clues.clue: The Protestant missionary Hudson Taylor founded the China Inland Mission in the country
examples: The main use of Moody's version of The Wordless Book was missionary work in this country, which he promoted after meeting Hudson Taylor, founder of its Inland Mission.

clues.clue: American traders operated in the country: Peter Parker served as a treaty translator before becoming a Western eye doctor there, James Flint was imprisoned three years despite securing the execution of agents who had extorted him, and Russell & Co. competed with the British Jardine, Matheson & Co., which nearly monopolized the opium trade
examples: Peter Parker served as a translator for one treaty the US signed with this country before becoming one of this country's first western eye doctors.
Despite securing the execution of agents who extorted him in this country, James Flint was imprisoned here for three years.

clues.clue: Revolutionary War veteran Samuel Shaw served as the first US consul, dispatched to Guangzhou in 1784; merchants at Salem, Massachusetts's East India Marine Society made fortunes trading silk with Qing merchants
examples: Name this dynasty to which Revolutionary War veteran Samuel Shaw served as consul when he was dispatched to Guangzhou in 1784.
Merchants at this New England city's East India Marine Society became millionaires from trading silk with Qing merchants.

clues.clue: The 1844 Treaty of Wanghia, negotiated by Caleb Cushing, was the first unequal treaty the US imposed on the dynasty, fixing tariffs and establishing extraterritoriality; the later Burlingame Treaty attempted a more equitable arrangement, and John Hay's Open Door policy urged Western powers to maintain equal trading access
examples: This 1844 treaty, negotiated by Caleb Cushing, was the first unequal treaty the US imposed on the Qing. This treaty fixed trade tariffs, allowed foreigners to learn Chinese, and established extraterritoriality clauses.
The Burlingame Treaty was an attempt to negotiate an equitable treaty with this country following an earlier one negotiated by Caleb Cushing.

clues.clue: After defeats in the Opium Wars, the dynasty was forced to sign a series of 'unequal treaties,' including those of Nanjing and Tianjin, and was later invaded by the multinational Eight-Power Alliance during the Boxer Rebellion
examples: After several humiliating military defeats, this dynasty was forced to sign "unequal treaties" such as those of Tianjin and Nanjing.
During its "Century of Humiliation", this country was invaded by the forces of the Eight-Power Alliance and forced to accept numerous "unequal treaties."

clues.clue: A political cartoon of the era's 'scramble for concessions' shows Bismarck glaring at Queen Victoria as he stabs into a pie representing the country
examples: A political cartoon about this country shows Bismarck glaring at Queen Victoria as he stabs into a pie.

clues.clue: Laborers who emigrated during the dynasty, including to the California gold rush, organized into mutual-aid kongsi ('company') organizations, which in Southeast Asia sometimes coalesced into self-governing states like the Lanfang Republic
examples: Kongsi were established to help laborers who emigrated during this last Imperial Chinese dynasty, including those who traveled to the California gold rush.

works.name: The Taiping Rebellion
works.description: The self-proclaimed brother of Jesus Christ, Hong Xiuquan, led the massive Taiping Rebellion against the dynasty, granting his generals fiefs with titles like the 'Western King of 8000 Years.' The dynasty's response included Zeng Guofan's reorganized Xiang Army and Li Hongzhang's push for military modernization.

clues.clue: Hong Xiuquan, a self-proclaimed brother of Jesus Christ, led the Taiping Rebellion and gave his major generals fiefs corresponding to the four cardinal directions with titles like the 'Western King of 8000 Years,' as recounted in Stephen R. Platt's Autumn in the Heavenly Kingdom, which argues that Hong Rengan played a pivotal role in the rebellion
examples: The leader of a rebellion against this dynasty gave fiefs corresponding to the four cardinal directions to his major generals, giving them titles like the "Western King of 8000 Years."
Platt argued that Hong Rengan played a pivotal role in that rebellion.

clues.clue: Li Hongzhang pushed for military modernization in response to the rebellion
examples: During this dynasty, military modernization was pushed for by Li Hongzhang.

clues.clue: Zeng Guofan reorganized the Xiang Army to fight the rebellion
examples: The Xiang army was reorganized by Zeng Guofan during this dynasty to fight a rebellion led by the self-proclaimed brother of Jesus, Hong Xiuquan.

works.name: Cixi, the Self-Strengthening Movement, and the Boxer Rebellion
works.description: The Empress Dowager Cixi regained power as regent after a coup ended the Guangxu Emperor's Hundred Days of Reform, later besieging the foreign Legation Quarter in support of the Boxer Rebellion. Her advisor Li Hongzhang sponsored the modernized Beiyang Fleet and negotiated the treaty ending the First Sino-Japanese War, while an eight-nation alliance intervened to suppress the Boxer Rebellion.

clues.clue: Cixi regained power as regent when a coup ended the Guangxu Emperor's Hundred Days of Reform; she diverted funds meant for new warships to rebuild the Summer Palace, and her military besieged the Legation Quarter in support of the Boxer Rebellion
examples: Name this ruler, who regained power as regent when a coup ended the Hundred Days of Reform initiated by the Guangxu Emperor. This ruler's military besieged the Legation Quarter in support of the Boxer Rebellion.
This ruler diverted funds meant for purchasing new warships to rebuild the Summer Palace.

clues.clue: Cixi's advisor Li Hongzhang sponsored the modernized Beiyang Fleet and, while negotiating the Treaty of Shimonoseki to end the First Sino-Japanese War, was wounded by a Japanese extremist
examples: Cixi was advised by this statesman, who sponsored the modernized Beiyang Fleet. While negotiating the Treaty of Shimonoseki to end the First Sino-Japanese War, he was wounded by a Japanese extremist.

clues.clue: An alliance of eight nations attacked the dynasty to suppress the Boxer Rebellion
examples: An alliance of this many nations attacked the Qing dynasty to suppress the Boxer Rebellion.

works.name: The Xinhai Revolution and the Fall of the Dynasty
works.description: The secret Tongmenghui organization was the driving force behind the Xinhai Revolution, which began with the Wuchang Uprising and ended with Puyi's abdication and the founding of the Republic of China. The anti-Manchu feminist revolutionary Qiu Jin, executed years earlier for plotting an uprising, was among the movement's forerunners.

clues.clue: The secret organization Tongmenghui was the 'brains' behind the Xinhai Revolution, which began on October 10 with the Wuchang Uprising; after its success, a debate arose over whether to replace the dynasty's dragon flag with the 'Five Races Under One Union' flag or the 'Blue Sky, White Sun' flag
examples: The Tongmenghui was a secret organization which was the brains behind this event, which began on October 10 in the year of the "iron pig" with the Wuchang Uprising.
After the success of this event, a debate began over whether to use the "Five Races Under One Union" flag or "Blue Sky, White Sun" flag to replace a flag featuring a dragon on a golden background.

clues.clue: British explorer Arthur de Carle Sowerby led a 600-mile trek to rescue missionaries during the revolution, whose precursors included 72 people killed in a southern-city uprising honored at the 'Yellow Flower Mound'; the last emperor, Puyi, abdicated after the revolution's success, establishing the Republic of China
examples: British explorer Arthur de Carle Sowerby led a 600-mile-long trek to the southwest during this event to rescue as many missionaries as possible. 72 people who launched a precursor to this event in a southern city are buried as martyrs in the "Yellow Flower Mound."
Puyi abdicated the throne after-for 10 points-what revolution that established the Republic of China?

clues.clue: The anti-Manchu feminist revolutionary Qiu Jin was executed for plotting the Anqing Uprising after publicly opposing foot-binding, the process of producing 'golden lotuses' begun in early childhood; her beheading in Shaoxing inspired the blood-soaked bun in Lu Xun's short story 'Medicine'
examples: Name this 19th-century Chinese anti-Manchu feminist leader. She was executed for plotting the Anqing Uprising.
Qiu Jin publicly opposed this custom that limited women's mobility in Imperial China. "Golden lotuses" were the ultimate goal of this process, which began in early childhood.

works.name: Art, Porcelain, and Literature
works.description: The dynasty's Jingdezhen kilns produced hallmark porcelain styles like famille rose and the copper-red 'peach bloom' glaze under the ceramist Tang Ying, while its painters ranged from the orthodox 'Four Wangs' to individualists like Shitao and the Jesuit court painter Giuseppe Castiglione. Its jade carvings, like the Jadeite Cabbage, and its popular fiction, including novels satirizing scholars, round out its cultural legacy.

clues.clue: Jingdezhen porcelain manufacture restarted under the Kangxi Emperor in the late 17th century, producing the short-lived copper-pigmented 'peach bloom' glaze and, developing from the preceding 'five colours' (wucai) style, the famille rose palette (called yangcai and falangcai) under the supervision of the writer and ceramist Tang Ying; undecorated wares were sent to Canton to be painted for export
examples: A short-lived trend during this period was the creation of works described as decorated with 'peach bloom', produced with a copper-rich pigment.
Tang noted that Yangcai styles had been introduced in imitation of falangcai, both styles from this dynasty which were described as famille rose porcelain by westerners.

clues.clue: The reticulated Bainbridge vase, decorated with panels of cavorting fish, was produced under Tang Ying's supervision
examples: A reticulated work from this period, decorated with panels of cavorting fish, is the Bainbridge vase.

clues.clue: The orthodox 'Four Wangs' were among the dynasty's 'Six Masters,' whose style contrasted with 'Individualists' like Shitao; one painting shows two houses nestled in trees degenerating into so-called '10,000 Ugly Inkblots,' while a self-taught artist born during the dynasty switched mid-career to a freehand style used for celebrated ink paintings of shrimp
examples: The Four Wangs were among the Six Masters of this dynasty whose orthodox style contrasted with "Individualists" like Shitao.
A self-taught artist born during this dynasty made a mid-career switch to a freehand style that he used for many celebrated ink paintings of shrimp.

clues.clue: The Jesuit Giuseppe Castiglione worked as a court painter, and later artists of the dynasty often illustrated the 'Twelve Beauties of Jinling' from the novel Dream of the Red Chamber, whose characters like Miaoyu and Lin Daiyu were common painterly subjects; artists like Qi Baishi learned composition from the dynasty's Manual of the Mustard Seed Garden
examples: Giuseppe Castiglione worked as a court painter during what dynasty, whose later artists often illustrated the Twelve Beauties of Jinling from Dream of the Red Chamber?
Painters like Qi Baishi learned composition from this dynasty's colorful Manual of the Mustard Seed Garden.

clues.clue: A jadeite carving of a cabbage with a locust and katydid, the Jadeite Cabbage, was first displayed during the dynasty, alongside the Meat-Shaped Stone, a jasper carving resembling braised pork belly
examples: The Jadeite Cabbage was first displayed during this last Chinese dynasty, when the Meat-Shaped Stone was sculpted.

clues.clue: Novels satirizing scholars, such as those of the 'talent and beauty' genre, became popular during the dynasty; Lu Xun wrote in the aftermath of its collapse in the 1910s
examples: Novels satirizing scholars, such as those of the "talent and beauty" genre, became popular during this final ruling Chinese dynasty. Writers such as Lu Xun wrote in the aftermath of this dynasty's collapse in the 1910s.

comprehensive_summary: The Qing dynasty (1644-1912), China's final imperial dynasty, grew out of the Jurchen chieftain Nurhaci's unification of his people, who were organized into the Eight Banners military system and issued his 'Seven Grievances' against the Ming before winning the Battle of Sarhu; Nurhaci was later wounded in a defeat at the Battle of Ningyuan and died soon after. His successor Hong Taiji renamed the Jurchens 'Manchu' and founded the dynasty proper, sending envoys to form the 'Alliance Conducted at Sea.' The conquest of China came in 1644, when the rebel Li Zicheng ('the Dashing Prince') sacked Beijing and the Ming general Wu Sangui let Manchu forces under the regent Prince Dorgon through Shanhai Pass to crush him; the new rulers then forced Han Chinese men to wear the queue hairstyle, provoking a two-and-a-half-month resistance in Jiangyin that ended in massacre. The Kangxi Emperor (r. 1661-1722) commissioned a namesake dictionary and encyclopedia, crushed the 1673 Revolt of the Three Feudatories led by the now-rebellious Wu Sangui alongside Geng Jingzhong and Shang Zhixin, revitalized the baojia household-surveillance system, signed the dynasty's first European treaty at Nerchinsk (1689, updated by the 1727 Treaty of Kyakhta), and began the conquest of the Dzungar Khanate under Galdan. His grandson the Qianlong Emperor restricted the Eight Banners to ethnic Manchus and, in his Ten Great Campaigns, completed the conquest of the Dzungars in what one genocide scholar called 'the 18th-century genocide par excellence' as part of a 'Pacification of Xinjiang' (later organized as a province in 1884 under the Guangxu Emperor), alongside less successful invasions of Burma and Vietnam; he employed the Jesuit Giuseppe Castiglione and Michel Benoist on a summer palace, hosted (and largely rejected) the 1793 Macartney Embassy under his corrupt favorite Heshen, and abdicated in 1796 to avoid surpassing Kangxi's reign length while ruling in fact until 1799. The dynasty's foreign relations were shaped by missionaries — Jesuits like Matteo Ricci sparking the Chinese Rites Controversy, and later Hudson Taylor's China Inland Mission — and by traders like Peter Parker, James Flint, and Russell & Co. (competing with the opium-trading Jardine, Matheson & Co.); the US consul Samuel Shaw arrived at Guangzhou in 1784, and Salem, Massachusetts merchants grew rich on the silk trade. After the Opium Wars, the dynasty signed a cascade of 'unequal treaties' — Wanghia (1844, negotiated by Caleb Cushing), Nanjing, and Tianjin — as depicted in a cartoon of Bismarck stabbing a pie representing China, while later attempts like the Burlingame Treaty and John Hay's Open Door policy sought more equitable footing; emigrant laborers, including those bound for the California gold rush, organized into mutual-aid kongsi. Domestically, the self-proclaimed brother of Jesus Christ, Hong Xiuquan, led the massive Taiping Rebellion, granting generals titles like the 'Western King of 8000 Years' (per Stephen R. Platt's Autumn in the Heavenly Kingdom, which credits Hong Rengan as pivotal), prompting Zeng Guofan's Xiang Army and Li Hongzhang's push for military modernization. Later, the Empress Dowager Cixi regained power as regent after a coup ended the Guangxu Emperor's Hundred Days of Reform, diverted naval funds to rebuild the Summer Palace, and besieged the Legation Quarter in support of the Boxer Rebellion, which an eight-nation alliance intervened to suppress; her advisor Li Hongzhang sponsored the Beiyang Fleet and negotiated the Treaty of Shimonoseki ending the First Sino-Japanese War. The dynasty finally fell in the Xinhai Revolution, masterminded by the secret Tongmenghui and begun with the Wuchang Uprising, following precursors like the failed uprising commemorated at the 'Yellow Flower Mound' and the execution of the anti-Manchu feminist revolutionary Qiu Jin; the last emperor, Puyi, abdicated, establishing the Republic of China. Culturally, the dynasty is remembered for Jingdezhen porcelain — the copper-pigmented 'peach bloom' glaze, the famille rose (yangcai/falangcai) palette developed under Tang Ying, and the reticulated Bainbridge vase — and for painting ranging from the orthodox 'Four Wangs' to individualists like Shitao and the court painter Castiglione, whose contemporaries illustrated the 'Twelve Beauties of Jinling' from Dream of the Red Chamber; its jade carvings include the Jadeite Cabbage and Meat-Shaped Stone, and its popular fiction included novels satirizing scholars in the 'talent and beauty' genre.
recursive_suggestions: Kangxi Emperor — recurring ruler with substantial independent material (Treaty of Nerchinsk, Dzungar wars, Revolt of the Three Feudatories); watch for overlap with this page's ruler section if he gains his own topic
Qianlong Emperor — recurring ruler with strong independent material (Ten Great Campaigns, Macartney Embassy, Heshen); category filter to History
Empress Dowager Cixi — recurring figure with independent presence (Hundred Days of Reform, Boxer Rebellion, Summer Palace); category filter to History
Wu Sangui — recurring figure spanning the Ming-Qing transition and the Revolt of the Three Feudatories; category filter to History
Taiping Rebellion — has independent narrative material (Hong Xiuquan, Zeng Guofan, Li Hongzhang) and could become its own topic with more presence
Xinhai Revolution — recurring event with independent material (Tongmenghui, Wuchang Uprising, Puyi's abdication); category filter to History
Boxer Rebellion — recurring event tied to Cixi and the Eight-Nation Alliance; could merit its own topic with more independent presence
Dzungar Khanate — recurring polity with independent material (Galdan, Battle of Ulan Butung); category filter to History
Qiu Jin — anti-Manchu feminist revolutionary with some independent material; category filter to History
Nurhaci — dynastic founder-figure with independent material (Seven Grievances, Battle of Sarhu, Eight Banners); category filter to History

Excluded: a tossup on the Qin Dynasty (explicitly rejects 'Qing Dynasty') and a tossup on Chinese Australians (a diaspora topic, not the dynasty itself) were discarded as off-topic; several bonus parts whose answers were unrelated entities (e.g., Pope Pius, the Hui people, the Chagatai language, kaozheng philology) were also excluded from this page's clues

links.text: Revolt of the Three Feudatories — Wikipedia
